stem: Which agency licenses and inspects medical possession and use of byproduct material in a non-Agreement State?

options.text: Food and Drug Administration
Nuclear Regulatory Commission
Department of Transportation
Environmental Protection Agency

explanation: The NRC regulates possession and medical use of byproduct material in non-Agreement States through licensing, inspection, and enforcement. The FDA regulates products and manufacturing practices, while the DOT regulates transportation.

stem: What is the principal regulatory role of an Agreement State?

options.text: It approves radioactive drugs and devices for interstate marketing after evaluating safety and clinical effectiveness
It certifies every Type B shipping package used nationally
It assumes specified NRC authority to license and regulate byproduct material within the state
It replaces all federal radiation regulations with voluntary guidance

explanation: Under an agreement authorized by the Atomic Energy Act, a state assumes portions of the NRC's regulatory authority. Its program remains subject to NRC review for adequacy and compatibility.

stem: What does the NRC evaluate through the Integrated Materials Performance Evaluation Program?

options.text: Whether radiopharmaceuticals used in Agreement States remain clinically effective for every approved indication
Whether a carrier charged the correct shipping rate
Whether a hospital's diagnostic images meet accreditation standards
Whether Agreement State radiation-control programs remain adequate and compatible

explanation: IMPEP is the NRC's framework for periodically evaluating Agreement State and NRC materials programs for public-health protection and regulatory compatibility. It is not a drug-approval or image-quality program.

stem: An associate radiation safety officer is appointed to manage selected tasks. Which responsibility remains with the radiation safety officer?

options.text: Implementing the radiation protection program
Performing every daily area survey personally
Preparing every patient dose personally
Signing every package receipt

explanation: The RSO may assign specific duties to an ARSO in writing, but cannot delegate the authority or responsibility for implementing the radiation protection program. Routine tasks may be performed by appropriately trained personnel.

stem: Which authority must management provide to the radiation safety officer?

options.text: Authority to approve new radiopharmaceuticals for commercial marketing without separate FDA review
Authority to identify problems, initiate corrective action, stop unsafe operations, and verify correction
Authority to waive occupational dose limits whenever an authorized user documents clinical necessity
Authority to replace the NRC during an inspection

explanation: The RSO needs organizational freedom, resources, and authority to identify safety problems, act on them, stop unsafe work, and verify corrective actions. The RSO cannot waive regulations or assume another agency's role.

stem: Which individual meets the basic definition of an authorized user?

options.text: Any nuclear medicine technologist who has completed annual safety training and works under general supervision
Any pharmacist employed by the hospital
A physician, dentist, or podiatrist named on the license for specified medical uses
The administrator who signs the radioactive-materials license

explanation: An authorized user is a qualified physician, dentist, or podiatrist identified for specified medical uses under the license. Supervised staff may perform delegated tasks but do not thereby become authorized users.

stem: Which task may an authorized user or authorized nuclear pharmacist delegate?

options.text: Final responsibility for ensuring that all delegated work complies with the radioactive-materials license
Authority to approve a new authorized user without required qualifications
Authority to alter an NRC dose limit
A specific medical-use or preparation task to a properly instructed and supervised individual

explanation: AUs and ANPs may delegate defined tasks when the individual is properly trained and supervised. Delegation of a task does not transfer the authorized individual's regulatory responsibility.

stem: Which individual must be named on a license authorizing a gamma stereotactic radiosurgery unit?

options.text: Authorized medical physicist
Authorized nuclear pharmacist
Radiologic technologist
Department of Transportation safety officer

explanation: An authorized medical physicist is required for specified device-based uses, including gamma stereotactic radiosurgery, teletherapy, photon-emitting remote afterloaders, and strontium-90 ophthalmic applicators. An authorized nuclear pharmacist prepares radioactive drugs, while an authorized user directs medical administration; neither title automatically substitutes for the AMP role.

stem: When is a radiation safety committee required?

options.text: Whenever a facility employs more than two technologists
When the license authorizes two or more different types of byproduct-material use
Only after a reportable medical event demonstrates that the existing radiation-safety program was inadequate
Only for a Type C broad-scope license

explanation: A licensee authorized for two or more different types of medical use must have an RSC to oversee the licensed uses. Staffing count and prior events do not determine the requirement.

stem: Which person is required on a radiation safety committee?

options.text: A representative from the Department of Transportation
Every technologist, nurse, and pharmacist who handles radioactive material anywhere in the institution
A management representative who is neither an authorized user nor the radiation safety officer
The facility's malpractice insurer

explanation: Required membership includes the RSO, an AU for each type of permitted use, a nursing representative, and a management representative who is neither an AU nor the RSO. Other members may be added as appropriate.

stem: Which broad-scope license is most typical of a large academic medical center with diverse uses and an RSC?

options.text: Type B
Type C
Master Materials License
Type A

explanation: Type A is the broadest broad-scope license and is typical of large academic programs. Type B programs are smaller and less diverse, while Type C programs use smaller quantities and are common in small research or nuclear cardiology settings.

stem: Which description best fits a Type C broad-scope license?

options.text: A small program needing flexibility to possess varied materials in limited quantities
A large academic program with the broadest range of medical uses
A federal organization operating multiple medical and industrial sites under a single NRC oversight structure
A commercial carrier transporting Type B packages

explanation: Type C broad-scope licenses provide flexibility for smaller programs that do not require large quantities, such as some nuclear cardiology clinics and small research laboratories. Type A is the largest and most diverse broad-scope category, and a Master Materials License applies to a federal organization operating multiple sites.

stem: What is a Master Materials License?

options.text: A state license limited to one private nuclear pharmacy
An NRC license allowing a federal organization to oversee byproduct-material use at multiple sites
An FDA authorization allowing one manufacturer to market all of its radiopharmaceuticals at multiple federal sites
A shipping certificate valid for every Type A package

explanation: An MML lets a federal organization administer permits, inspections, and enforcement for multiple sites under its jurisdiction, while the NRC retains oversight and inspects the MML program at least every 2 years. It is a regulatory structure, not FDA approval of a manufacturer's products or a state broad-scope license.

stem: What is a written directive?

options.text: A technologist's note that a diagnostic study was completed
A carrier's record of a package transport index
An authorized user's written order for a specified administration to a patient or research subject
An annual management statement defining the facility's ALARA goals, investigational levels, and audit schedule

explanation: A written directive is the authorized user's written order for administration of byproduct material or radiation from byproduct material to a specific patient or human research subject. It is distinct from routine charting and shipping documentation.

stem: Which use of unsealed byproduct material ordinarily does not require a written directive?

options.text: Oral administration of iodine-131 above 30 microcuries
Parenteral administration of an alpha-emitting therapeutic drug
Administration of a therapeutic activity that requires a written directive
An uptake, dilution, or excretion study using an appropriately prepared radioactive drug

explanation: Uptake, dilution, and excretion studies ordinarily do not require a written directive. Therapeutic unsealed-material uses and oral iodine-131 above the specified activity do require one.

stem: What training is required for an authorized user performing uptake, dilution, and excretion studies under the described pathway?

options.text: 60 hours total, including at least 8 hours of classroom and laboratory training
80 hours total, all of which must be classroom training
700 hours total, including at least 200 classroom hours
Only the facility's annual in-service training

explanation: The uptake, dilution, and excretion pathway requires 60 hours of training and experience, including at least 8 classroom and laboratory hours, plus supervised work experience, examination, and attestation requirements. The much larger 700-hour pathways apply to imaging/localization or therapeutic written-directive uses.

stem: What training is required for the imaging and localization authorized-user pathway?

options.text: 60 hours total, including 8 classroom hours
700 hours total, including at least 80 hours of classroom and laboratory training
700 hours total, including 200 classroom hours
80 hours total with no supervised work experience

explanation: Imaging and localization require 700 hours of training and experience with at least 80 classroom and laboratory hours. The 200-classroom-hour requirement applies to the broader therapeutic written-directive pathway.

stem: Which training requirement applies to use of unsealed byproduct material that requires a written directive?

options.text: 700 hours of training and experience, including 80 classroom hours
60 hours of training and experience, including 8 classroom hours
700 hours of training and experience, including 200 classroom hours
Annual safety instruction only

explanation: The therapeutic written-directive pathway requires 700 hours, including 200 classroom hours, along with appropriate residency or board pathways, supervised experience, examination, and attestation. Imaging and localization also require 700 total hours but only 80 classroom and laboratory hours, which is the key distinction.

stem: For an authorized user seeking approval for a therapeutic administration category, how much case experience is required under the described alternate pathway?

options.text: At least 1 case in any category
At least 10 cases total
No case experience if an attestation is signed
At least 3 cases in each requested category

explanation: The individual must have supervised experience with at least three cases in each requested administration category. Written attestation complements rather than replaces the required case experience.

stem: A 42-year-old patient is scheduled for therapeutic iodine-131 and has no history of hysterectomy or bilateral tubal ligation. What must be documented?

options.text: A negative serum beta-hCG result obtained within 1 week before therapy
A negative urine test obtained within 30 days
A declaration that the patient is not breastfeeding, with no pregnancy test
No pregnancy evaluation if the patient denies pregnancy

explanation: Potentially childbearing individuals under 50 require a documented negative serum beta-hCG within one week of iodine-131 therapy unless a stated exception such as hysterectomy or remote bilateral tubal ligation applies. A verbal denial of pregnancy or an older urine test does not meet the stated documentation requirement.

stem: At what oral iodine-131 activity is a written directive required?

options.text: Greater than 1 millicurie
Greater than 30 microcuries
Only greater than 33 millicuries
Only when the patient will be hospitalized

explanation: A written directive signed and dated by an authorized user is required for oral iodine-131 above 1.11 MBq, or 30 microcuries. The 33-mCi value distinguishes training categories, not the basic directive threshold.

stem: Which room preparation is appropriate before inpatient iodine-131 therapy?

options.text: Keep radioactive waste containers outside the room so housekeeping can remove them without entering
Place linen and trash hampers in the hallway
Post radiation and no-housekeeping signs, place waste containers inside, and cover frequently touched surfaces
Leave the room unchanged and survey only after discharge

explanation: The room is prepared before dosing to control contamination: post the required signs, retain linen and waste inside, and cover frequently touched surfaces. These controls reduce spread and unnecessary staff exposure.

stem: A hospitalized iodine-131 therapy patient develops cardiac arrest. What is the first priority?

options.text: Wait for the patient to meet release criteria so emergency staff are not exposed during resuscitation
Complete a contamination survey before beginning CPR
Move all emergency equipment out of the room
Provide emergency medical care using appropriate barriers and facility protocols

explanation: Immediate life-saving care takes priority. Staff should use barriers and radiation-safety precautions when feasible, but restrictions should not delay treatment of imminent death or severe harm.

stem: Which visitor policy is appropriate for a hospitalized iodine-131 therapy patient?

options.text: Exclude pregnant visitors and minors, and limit duration while maximizing distance
Permit unrestricted visits by adults if they wear a surgical mask and remain on the opposite side of the bed
Allow minors but prohibit adults older than 65
Require all visitors to remain within 1 meter for accurate monitoring

explanation: Typical controls prohibit pregnant visitors and visitors younger than 18, limit visits to about 30 minutes, and maintain approximately 6 feet of distance. Local rules may be more restrictive.

stem: A daily survey measures 7 mrem/hour at 1 meter from an inpatient iodine-131 patient's chest. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: It requires continued hospitalization until the reading falls below 2 mrem per hour at the room doorway
It generally meets the dose-rate criterion for discharge, subject to facility and state requirements
It exceeds the Yellow-III package limit
It permits discharge only if no written instructions are provided

explanation: A reading of 7 mrem/hour or less at 1 meter is generally consistent with the iodine-131 release criterion cited in the document. Release still requires assessment of likely dose to others and applicable local requirements.

stem: When may a patient containing radioactive material be released from licensee control?

options.text: Only after all measurable activity has decayed to background at both the patient's surface and 1 meter
Whenever the patient requests discharge
When dose to any other individual is not likely to exceed 500 mrem, or an applicable activity or dose-rate criterion is met
Only when the dose rate at contact is below background

explanation: Release is based on limiting likely dose to another person to 500 mrem (5 mSv), with radionuclide-specific activity or dose-rate criteria available as alternatives. Zero measurable activity is not required.

stem: When must a released patient receive written instructions for limiting dose to others?

options.text: Only when another individual's dose could exceed 500 mrem
Only after iodine-131 therapy above 33 mCi
Whenever any diagnostic tracer is administered
When another individual's dose could exceed 100 mrem (1 mSv)

explanation: Written ALARA instructions are required if dose to another person could exceed 100 mrem. The 500-mrem value is the upper release criterion, so the instruction threshold is intentionally lower.

stem: How must licensed material in a hot lab or imaging area be secured?

options.text: In a locked area or under constant surveillance
By posting a radiation sign and maintaining a written inventory even when the area remains unlocked
By storing it beside the dose calibrator
By documenting it in the annual audit

explanation: Licensed material must be protected against unauthorized access, either in locked storage or under constant surveillance. A sign or inventory record alone does not physically secure it.

stem: How often must personnel caring for patients who cannot be released receive radiation-safety instruction?

options.text: Every 3 years, coordinated with retention of written-directive and survey records
Initially and at least annually
Only after an incident or a substantial change in the patient's treatment plan
Once at initial employment, with later instruction required only for authorized users

explanation: Instruction is required initially and at least annually. It covers patient and visitor control, contamination and waste, and notification of the RSO and authorized user during an emergency or death.

stem: Which arrangement is acceptable for a patient who cannot be released after unsealed-material therapy?

options.text: Any semiprivate room if a portable shield separates the patient from an untreated roommate
A public waiting area with a portable shield
A private room with a private sanitary facility, or a room shared with another unreleasable therapy patient
A room shared with an unexposed pediatric patient

explanation: The patient should occupy a private room with a private sanitary facility or share only with another patient who received similar therapy and also cannot be released. Room signage and visitor controls are also required.

stem: An item is removed from the room of a patient who cannot be released. How should it be handled?

options.text: Release it after wiping it with disinfectant if no visible liquid or patient-identifying material remains
Treat it as ordinary waste if it has no visible contamination
Store it for exactly 24 hours regardless of radionuclide
Survey it and release it only if indistinguishable from background, or manage it as radioactive waste

explanation: Items leaving the room must be monitored; if radioactivity is distinguishable from background, they are controlled as radioactive waste. Visual inspection or a fixed waiting period is not a substitute for measurement.

stem: What is the maximum molybdenum-99 breakthrough permitted in technetium-99m eluate for human administration?

options.text: 0.15 microcurie of molybdenum-99 per millicurie of technetium-99m
0.02 microcurie of molybdenum-99 per millicurie of technetium-99m
0.2 microcurie of molybdenum-99 per millicurie of technetium-99m
1 microcurie of molybdenum-99 per millicurie of technetium-99m

explanation: The molybdenum-99 limit is 0.15 microcurie per millicurie of technetium-99m. The 0.02 and 0.2 values apply to strontium-82 and strontium-85 breakthrough, respectively, in rubidium-82 eluate.

stem: When should strontium-82 and strontium-85 breakthrough be measured for a rubidium-82 generator?

options.text: After the last patient of the week
Before the first patient of the day
Only when image quality deteriorates
At annual license renewal

explanation: Parent and contaminant strontium concentrations are checked before the first patient each day. This prevents administration of eluate that exceeds breakthrough limits. Waiting until the end of the day would allow every patient that day to receive an unverified product.

stem: An eluate exceeds an applicable radionuclide breakthrough limit. What reporting is required?

options.text: Telephone reporting by the next calendar day followed by a written report within 15 calendar days
A written report at the next annual audit
Telephone reporting within 7 calendar days and a written report within 30 calendar days
No report if no patient received the eluate

explanation: The licensee reports the excessive measurement by telephone within 7 calendar days and submits a written report within 30 days. The written report addresses corrective action, cause, and patient dose assessment when applicable.

stem: How long must the licensee retain records of RSO authority and radiation-protection-program changes?

options.text: 1 year
3 years
Only until the next inspection
5 years

explanation: Records documenting RSO authority and radiation-program changes are retained for 5 years. Many operational records, including written directives, surveys, calibrations, dosage determinations, and leak tests, are retained for 3 years.

stem: Which record is generally retained for 3 years?

options.text: A written directive
The original radioactive-materials license only until renewal
An RSO program-change record
An employee's Social Security card

explanation: Copies of written directives are retained for 3 years, as are many calibration, survey, dosage, release, leak-test, and inventory records. RSO authority and program-change records have a 5-year retention period.

stem: Licensed material is discovered missing, and exposure to people in unrestricted areas appears possible. What is required?

options.text: An internal incident report followed by notification only if a member of the public later receives a confirmed dose
Prompt telephone notification to the NRC followed by a written report within 30 days
A written report after the next semiannual inventory
Notification only if a member of the public reports symptoms

explanation: Potentially consequential lost, stolen, or missing licensed material requires telephone reporting, followed by a detailed written report within 30 days describing the material, circumstances, possible exposures, recovery efforts, and preventive measures. An internal incident report alone is insufficient when exposure in unrestricted areas appears possible.

stem: A licensee learns that an occupational or public dose limit was exceeded. By when is the written regulatory report required?

options.text: Within 24 hours
Within 7 days
Within 30 days
At the next license renewal

explanation: The document requires a written report within 30 days after learning of an excess occupational, fetal, minor, or public dose, as well as specified excessive emissions or area radiation levels. The deadline differs from next-day telephone reporting used for certain medical or fetal events.
